records.ety: [OE. shrinken, schrinken, AS. scrincan; akin to OD. schrincken, and probably to Sw. skrynka a wrinkle, skrynkla to wrinkle, to rumple, and E. shrimp, n. & v., scrimp. CF. Shrimp.]
records.morph: [imp. Shrank (?) or Shrunk (?) p. p. Shrunk or Shrunken (<?/), but the latter is now seldom used except as a participial adjective; p. pr. & vb. n. Shrinking.]

defs: To wrinkle, bend, or curl; to shrivel; hence, to contract into a less extent or compass; to gather together; to become compacted.
quotes: And on a broken reed he still did stay His feeble steps, which shrunk when hard thereon he lay.
I have not found that water, by mixture of ashes, will shrink or draw into less room.
Against this fire do I shrink up.
And shrink like parchment in consuming fire.

defs: To withdraw or retire, as from danger; to decline action from fear; to recoil, as in fear, horror, or distress.
quotes: What happier natures shrink at with affright, The hard inhabitant contends is right.
They assisted us against the Thebans when you shrank from the task.

defs: To express fear, horror, or pain by contracting the body, or part of it; to shudder; to quake.
To cause to contract or shrink; as, to shrink finnel by immersing it in boiling water.

quotes: The Libyc Hammon shrinks his horn.

records.ety: [Contraction of head-shrinker, a colloquial term for psychiatrist.]

defs: The act shrinking; shrinkage; contraction; also, recoil; withdrawal.
quotes: Yet almost wish, with sudden shrink, That I had less to praise.
